checkpoint_context.active_numbering_note: The active checkpoint path authorizes HOTSWAP_BOUNDARIES-0 after the post-Υ-C review while Φ-B5 remains later and premature.

hotswap_definition.summary: Hotswap is the live replacement or live handoff of a bounded runtime or control-plane realization across an explicit boundary while preserving declared identity, lifecycle legality, state continuity, replay intelligibility, snapshot legality, isolation posture, coexistence legality, and any required release-control, receipt, and trust continuity.
why_needed: The repo already contains explicit lifecycle, replay, snapshot, isolation, coexistence, release-control, rehearsal, canary, downgrade, and trust doctrine that could otherwise be misread as implicit hotswap permission.
Live replacement changes continuity and authority assumptions in ways that cannot be derived from coexistence or release convenience alone.
Later distributed-authority and Zeta planning need one explicit boundary model instead of implementation-specific reload folklore.

hotswap_subject_classes.description: Semantic truth owners, authority-bearing domain structures, bridge-legality owners, and semantic contract meaning owners.
Kernel-adjacent and continuity-defining runtime subjects whose live replacement would alter lifecycle, replay, or snapshot meaning.
Service-facing realizations whose authoritative truth is externalized and whose lifecycle, replay, snapshot, and isolation posture remain explicit.
Observation-facing, representation-facing, or projection-facing realizations that own no authoritative truth and do not redefine semantic meaning.
Runtime-adjacent control-plane helpers whose local replacement must not alter release truth, receipt continuity, or trust posture.
Trust-root, revocation-sensitive, or distributed-authority subjects whose live transition legality depends on later doctrine.

boundary_classes.description: Live replacement is unlawful because semantic truth, authority, or bridge legality would be altered.
Replacement may occur only through stop, suspend, retire, or restart semantics rather than live cutover.
Narrow local replacement may be discussable for bounded non-truth-owning subjects without claiming full runtime continuity.
Live replacement is discussable only with explicit lifecycle, state, replay, snapshot, isolation, coexistence, receipt, and trust review.
The subject depends on unsolved live-cutover, trust-rotation, or distributed-authority work and remains outside the current maturity floor.

distinction_notes.hotswap: Live replacement or live handoff across an explicit continuity boundary.
distinction_notes.restart: A non-live continuity break or restart posture rather than a live swap.
distinction_notes.rollback: A governed restoration or reselection transaction, not automatically a runtime live replacement.
distinction_notes.downgrade: A downward release-control move that remains distinct from live runtime cutover.
distinction_notes.multi_version_coexistence: Bounded simultaneous presence or resolvability under explicit law, not live replacement.
distinction_notes.archive_retention: Historical preservation without runtime swappability authority.
distinction_notes.publication_visibility_change: Feed or mirror exposure change without runtime cutover meaning.
distinction_notes.implementation_level_hot_reload: A tool-local reload mechanism that does not define canonical hotswap law.

invalidity_failure_categories.description: The boundary is explicit and the declared continuity envelope is satisfied.
Stronger hotswap claims require explicit human review.
Replacement may be legal only through non-live restart-class semantics.
Some evidence exists, but proof of continuity remains incomplete.
The subject is too authority-bearing or semantically binding to be hot-swapped lawfully.
Lifecycle posture is incompatible with the claimed live replacement.
Required state is trapped, hidden, or unlawfully intertwined.
Replay intelligibility or snapshot legality would be broken.
Isolation or containment boundaries are insufficient.
Coexistence exists, but it does not satisfy live replacement constraints.
Release-profile, operator, receipt, or provenance continuity is incomplete.
Trust posture or revocation continuity blocks the live claim.
The boundary depends on later doctrine or remains prohibited by current policy.
